_comment: Labeled NCD calibration/benchmark corpus for Epic 13.0. 'duplicates' are pairs reporting the SAME finding in entirely different vocabulary (token-set Jaccard scores them distinct; NCD must merge them). 'distinct' are pairs that are NOT the same finding (NCD must not merge them). Each finding carries problem/fix/evidence — the three text columns NCD scores together. Used by ncd_corpus_test.go (calibration) and ncd_bench_test.go (AC3 throughput).

a.problem: the jwt access token issued at login is never invalidated server side when the user logs out
a.fix: maintain a server side revocation list or use short lived tokens with refresh rotation
a.evidence: the logout handler clears the client cookie but never calls the token store

b.problem: after a person signs off from their account the previously granted bearer credential keeps working
b.fix: introduce a deny list of retired credentials or rotate the refresh secret at sign off
b.evidence: the sign out routine wipes only the browser state while the bearer still passes verification

a.problem: user supplied search text is concatenated directly into the sql statement allowing injection
a.fix: switch to parameterized queries with bound placeholders instead of string building
a.evidence: the where clause is assembled with fmt sprintf over the raw request parameter

b.problem: the report filter endpoint interpolates request input into a database command without escaping
b.fix: use prepared statements so the driver binds arguments separately from the query
b.evidence: the handler builds the lookup by joining strings that include untrusted form fields

a.problem: the response body is dereferenced without checking whether the upstream call returned nil
a.fix: guard the pointer and return early when the upstream response is nil
a.evidence: calling code reads resp body immediately after a fetch that can fail and yield nil

b.problem: a null pointer is possible because the function uses the result before confirming it exists
b.fix: add a presence check on the returned object before accessing its members
b.evidence: the value from the network helper is used straight away even though that helper may hand back nothing

a.problem: a new goroutine is spawned per request but nothing ever signals it to stop so they accumulate
a.fix: attach a context with cancellation and have the worker exit when the context is done
a.evidence: the spawn site has no wait group or cancel and the count climbs under sustained load

b.problem: background workers are started without any shutdown path which slowly exhausts memory
b.fix: thread a cancelable context through and return from the loop once cancellation fires
b.evidence: profiling shows the live routine total only ever increasing while traffic continues

a.problem: the authorization header is written to the application log in clear text on every request
a.fix: redact or omit sensitive headers before emitting any log line
a.evidence: the request logging middleware dumps the full header map including the bearer token

b.problem: api credentials end up in the log output because the whole payload is printed for debugging
b.fix: scrub secret fields from structures before they reach the logger
b.evidence: a debug statement serializes the entire config object which contains the access key

a.problem: a shared map is read and written from multiple goroutines without any synchronization
a.fix: protect the map with a mutex or switch to sync map for concurrent access
a.evidence: the cache is mutated in a handler while a background refresher iterates the same map

b.problem: concurrent access to the in memory store is unguarded so the data structure can corrupt
b.fix: wrap every read and write in a lock to serialize access to the structure
b.evidence: the race detector flags simultaneous entry updates from request threads and the sweeper

a.problem: the pagination offset is computed as page times size which skips the first record on page one
a.fix: use page minus one times size so the first page starts at the correct index
a.evidence: requesting the first page omits the earliest row and the last row is duplicated

b.problem: list paging drops one item at the boundary because the start index is off by a single position
b.fix: correct the index math so each page begins exactly where the previous one ended
b.evidence: users report the top entry missing whenever they open the initial results screen

a.problem: the username field has no maximum length so an attacker can submit megabytes of data
a.fix: enforce a sensible upper bound on the field length before processing
a.evidence: the handler accepts and stores arbitrarily large strings straight into the database

b.problem: there is no cap on the size of the supplied name which enables a denial of service
b.fix: reject inputs over a reasonable character limit early in request handling
b.evidence: posting an enormous value for the field causes the row insert to balloon unchecked

a.problem: the error returned by the write call is ignored so failures pass silently
a.fix: check the returned error and propagate or log it instead of discarding it
a.evidence: the save function assigns the error to the blank identifier and continues

b.problem: a failure from the persistence layer is dropped because the return value is never inspected
b.fix: handle the returned status so a failed operation does not look successful
b.evidence: calling code discards what the store hands back and always reports success to the caller

a.problem: passwords are hashed with a single round of md5 which is trivially crackable
a.fix: use a slow adaptive hash such as bcrypt or argon2 with a per user salt
a.evidence: the account creation path stores md5 of the raw password with no salt at all

b.problem: the credential storage relies on a fast unsalted digest that offers little protection
b.fix: migrate to a memory hard key derivation function with a unique salt per record
b.evidence: stored secrets are a plain hexadecimal sha of the secret making rainbow tables viable

a.problem: the download endpoint joins the user supplied name onto a base path allowing directory traversal
a.fix: sanitize the path and confirm the resolved location stays within the allowed root
a.evidence: requesting dot dot slash sequences reads files outside the intended folder

b.problem: a client controlled filename is used to build the served path which permits escaping the directory
b.fix: clean the requested path and reject any resolution that leaves the served root
b.evidence: supplying parent directory tokens in the name exposes arbitrary host files

a.problem: date subtraction uses local time so results shift by an hour across daylight saving changes
a.fix: perform the arithmetic in utc and convert to local only for display
a.evidence: the elapsed days count is wrong for any interval that crosses a clock change

b.problem: the duration calculation mixes wall clock zones which produces incorrect spans twice a year
b.fix: normalize both timestamps to a single absolute zone before computing the difference
b.evidence: reports near the spring transition show an interval that is sixty minutes short

a.problem: the server reflects the request origin and allows credentials which defeats cross origin protection
a.fix: restrict allowed origins to an explicit allow list and avoid echoing arbitrary values
a.evidence: the access control allow origin header is set to whatever the caller sends

b.problem: cross origin policy is effectively disabled because every site is permitted with cookies
b.fix: configure a fixed set of trusted domains rather than accepting all of them
b.evidence: any external page can read authenticated responses because the wildcard is paired with credentials

a.problem: the allocation size is computed by multiplying two request values which can overflow and wrap
a.fix: validate the factors and use checked multiplication before allocating
a.evidence: large width and height inputs produce a tiny buffer that is later overrun

b.problem: a product of user numbers is used as a buffer length and wraps around to a small value
b.fix: bound each operand and detect overflow prior to reserving memory
b.evidence: supplying big dimensions yields a wrapped total so the following copy writes past the end

a.problem: the client retries failed requests immediately in a tight loop hammering the upstream
a.fix: add exponential backoff with jitter between attempts and a maximum retry cap
a.evidence: on an outage the code reissues the call with no delay thousands of times a second

b.problem: failed calls are repeated instantly with no pause which amplifies load during incidents
b.fix: space attempts using growing delays and randomization plus an upper bound
b.evidence: when the dependency is down the retry path spins continuously without any wait

a.problem: the lookup filters on a column that has no index so every query scans the whole table
a.fix: add an index on the filtered column to avoid full table scans
a.evidence: explain shows a sequential scan over millions of rows for a single user fetch

b.problem: queries are slow because the searched field is not indexed forcing a complete scan
b.fix: create an index covering the predicate so the planner can seek instead of scanning
b.evidence: the query plan reports reading the entire relation to satisfy an equality filter

a.problem: the database password is embedded directly in the source file and committed to the repo
a.fix: load the secret from the environment or a secrets manager at runtime
a.evidence: the connection string literal contains the production password in plaintext

b.problem: an api key is baked into the code instead of being supplied through configuration
b.fix: read the key from an injected environment variable rather than a constant
b.evidence: a string constant near the top of the client holds the live service token

a.problem: user comment text is rendered into the page without escaping enabling stored cross site scripting
a.fix: html escape all user content on output or use a templating layer that auto escapes
a.evidence: a comment containing a script tag executes when other users view the thread

b.problem: profile fields are written into markup verbatim which lets attackers inject active scripts
b.fix: encode dynamic values before they are placed in the document body
b.evidence: setting a bio with an inline event handler runs javascript for every visitor

a.problem: the file handle is closed in two places on the error path which panics on the second close
a.fix: close exactly once using a single deferred close guarded against repetition
a.evidence: the cleanup branch and the deferred call both invoke close on the same descriptor

b.problem: a connection can be released twice when an early return also triggers the deferred cleanup
b.fix: ensure the resource is freed a single time by removing the redundant manual release
b.evidence: under the failure branch the socket close runs once inline and again from the defer

a.problem: the login flow redirects to a url taken from a query parameter without validation enabling open redirect
a.fix: allow only relative paths or check the target against an approved host list
a.evidence: setting the next parameter to an external site sends users there after authenticating

b.problem: a post action forwards the browser to a caller supplied address which phishers can abuse
b.fix: restrict the forward destination to known internal routes and reject absolute urls
b.evidence: the return to field accepts a full external link and the handler trusts it blindly

a.problem: the jwt access token issued at login is never invalidated server side when the user logs out
a.fix: maintain a server side revocation list or use short lived tokens with refresh rotation
a.evidence: the logout handler clears the client cookie but never calls the token store

b.problem: the responsive grid on the dashboard overflows its container on very narrow mobile viewports
b.fix: add a min width media query and collapse the grid to a single column under four hundred pixels
b.evidence: chrome devtools at three twenty pixels shows the grid exceeding the viewport width

distinct.note: sql injection vs slow query (same area, different issue)
